While platforms have historically been left to self-regulate youth access, the emerging consensus across multiple continents suggests that era may be ending. The central tension — balancing child safety against privacy concerns and the practical enforceability of age verification — will likely define tech policy debates for years to come.

articles.headline: Anthropic Takes Pentagon to Court Over Supply-Chain Risk Label
articles.subheadline: CEO Dario Amodei calls the DOD designation 'legally unsound' as OpenAI moves to fill the void in military AI contracts
articles.body:
Anthropic announced Thursday it will mount a legal challenge against the Department of Defense's decision to designate the AI company a supply-chain risk — a label that effectively bars it from Pentagon contracts and could ripple through its relationships with military contractors.

The confrontation marks a dramatic escalation in what began as a policy disagreement between the Claude developer and the U.S. military over the boundaries of AI deployment. Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei has drawn a hard line against allowing the company's models to be used for mass surveillance of American citizens or fully autonomous weapons systems, while the Pentagon insisted on unrestricted access for "all lawful purposes." The DOD formalized its supply-chain risk designation on Thursday, hours before Amodei issued his statement calling the legal basis for the decision unsound.

Amodei sought to limit the fallout by arguing the designation is narrow in scope. He contended that the label applies only to the use of Claude as a direct component of DOD contracts — not to broader commercial relationships between Anthropic and companies that happen to also do business with the Pentagon. Federal law, he noted, requires the Secretary of Defense to employ "the least restrictive means necessary" to protect the military supply chain. The vast majority of Anthropic's customer base, he said, remains unaffected. Meanwhile, Amodei confirmed that Anthropic is continuing to support certain U.S. military operations in Iran and would provide its models to the DOD at "nominal cost" during any transition period, underscoring the company's reluctance to frame the dispute as adversarial to national security.

The conflict deepened after an internal Amodei memo was leaked in which he characterized rival OpenAI's military dealings as "safety theater." OpenAI has since signed a deal to replace Anthropic in Pentagon work, a move that has reportedly generated backlash among OpenAI's own staff and contributed to a 295 percent surge in ChatGPT uninstalls. Amodei apologized for the memo's tone on Thursday, calling it a product of "a difficult day for the company" that no longer reflects his considered views.

Legal experts suggest Anthropic faces steep odds in court. The law governing supply-chain risk designations limits traditional avenues for challenging government procurement decisions and grants the Pentagon wide latitude on national security matters. Dean Ball, a former Trump-era White House AI adviser who has criticized Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's handling of the situation, acknowledged that "courts are pretty reluctant to second-guess the government on what is and is not a national security issue," though he added that success is "not impossible." Any challenge would likely be filed in federal court in Washington, setting the stage for a landmark case at the intersection of AI ethics, corporate autonomy, and military authority.

articles.headline: Civilian Toll Mounts as US-Israeli Strikes Hit Iranian Schools, Hospital
articles.subheadline: Verified satellite imagery reveals damage to UNESCO heritage sites, stadiums, and a hospital as reported deaths surpass 1,100
articles.body:
Schools, a hospital, historic landmarks, and sporting complexes have sustained severe damage since United States and Israeli military strikes against Iran began on Saturday, according to verified satellite imagery and video footage analyzed by BBC Verify, as the reported civilian death toll climbs past 1,100.

The US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency (HRANA) reports 1,168 civilians killed, including 194 children, though the full scale of destruction remains obscured by an almost total internet blackout imposed by Iranian authorities. Among the most devastating single incidents, 168 people were killed Saturday morning when a school in the southern city of Minab was struck. US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said the blast is under investigation, insisting that American forces "never target civilian targets." A separate school in Urmia, in northwestern Iran, also sustained confirmed damage.

In Tehran, Gandhi Hospital was hit on Sunday, with verified footage showing extensive structural damage, debris raining down, and patients — including a baby in an incubator — being evacuated. Iran's Medical Council chief said the hospital's IVF department was destroyed. The Israel Defense Forces acknowledged "minor damage" to the hospital from a strike on a nearby "military facility," noting the hospital sits opposite studios belonging to state broadcaster IRIB. The World Health Organization called the incident "extremely worrying," reiterating that health facilities are protected under international humanitarian law. Iran's foreign ministry labeled the attacks a "blatant war crime."

The damage extends well beyond medical and educational facilities. At least three major sporting complexes in Tehran were struck Thursday night — described by residents as the most intense bombardment yet — including the 12,000-seat Azadi indoor stadium, which satellite images show nearly collapsed. Israel's foreign ministry claimed the facility was being used as a "resting place" for Iranian forces. Tehran's centuries-old Grand Bazaar and the Golestan Palace, a UNESCO World Heritage site and former royal residence, both sustained damage from nearby strikes targeting a judicial complex. UNESCO confirmed it had provided coordinates of protected sites to all parties and stressed that cultural property is shielded under international law.

The humanitarian crisis is deepening rapidly. The United Nations refugee agency reported Wednesday that approximately 100,000 people fled Tehran within the first 48 hours, with 1,000 to 2,000 vehicles leaving the capital daily, mostly heading north. Tehran residents who managed to connect through the internet blackout described the city as a "ghost town" reeking of gunpowder, with one calling Thursday night "hell on earth." Legal experts cautioned that while strikes on civilian sites are illegal under international law unless those sites serve military purposes, exceptions for incidental damage to protected structures can create what Michael Becker, assistant professor of law at Trinity College Dublin, described as "very weak constraints on military decision-making." With the nationwide internet blackout still largely in effect, according to monitoring group Netblocks, the full scope of civilian harm may not be known for some time.

articles.headline: European Stocks Suffer Worst Weekly Decline Since April Amid Iran War
articles.subheadline: Escalating conflict with no resolution in sight rattles investors and drags down markets across the continent
articles.body:
European equities closed out their sharpest weekly selloff since April as the ongoing war in Iran continued to weigh heavily on investor sentiment, with no diplomatic resolution appearing imminent.

The broad decline, reported on March 6, 2026, reflected deepening anxiety across continental markets that the conflict could further disrupt global energy supplies, trade routes, and the broader geopolitical landscape. Defense and energy stocks have been among the most volatile sectors in recent weeks, while risk-sensitive assets bore the brunt of the selling pressure as traders moved toward safer havens.

The selloff underscores how the Iran conflict has evolved from a regional crisis into a persistent drag on global financial markets. European economies, with their significant dependence on Middle Eastern energy imports and exposure to global supply chain disruptions, are particularly vulnerable to prolonged instability in the region. The absence of credible ceasefire talks or diplomatic breakthroughs has left market participants with little reason to bet on a near-term recovery.

The weekly drop marks a notable shift in tone for European markets, which had enjoyed a period of relative stability in the months following last April's turbulence. Analysts noted that the escalation has revived concerns about stagflation — the toxic combination of slowing growth and persistent inflation — that had begun to recede from investor forecasts. Rising oil prices tied to the conflict have added fresh fuel to inflation fears, complicating the European Central Bank's path on monetary policy.

With geopolitical risk now firmly back at the center of market calculus, investors are bracing for continued volatility. The situation in Iran remains fluid, and until a credible path toward de-escalation emerges, European equities are likely to remain under pressure as portfolio managers reassess their risk exposure across the region.

articles.headline: Hundreds of Thousands Flee as Israel Launches Sweeping Strikes on South Beirut
articles.subheadline: Unprecedented evacuation orders displace massive populations; shelters overwhelmed as Lebanon's PM warns of looming humanitarian disaster
articles.body:
Israel carried out what it described as a "broad-scale wave" of airstrikes on the Dahieh suburb of southern Beirut overnight into Friday, targeting what it said was a Hezbollah command center and a facility storing unmanned aerial vehicles. The strikes came alongside sweeping evacuation orders covering huge portions of southern Lebanon and the capital's southern suburbs — orders unprecedented in scale, affecting hundreds of thousands of civilians and triggering scenes of mass displacement, gridlocked roads, and families sleeping on streets and in cars.

The escalation marks a dramatic resumption of full-scale hostilities just months after a ceasefire ended 13 months of war between Israel and Hezbollah in November 2024. Israel had continued near-daily strikes on Lebanese targets since that agreement, claiming Hezbollah was attempting to reconstitute its military capabilities. Israel's far-right finance minister Bezalel Smotrich inflamed tensions further on Thursday by declaring that Dahieh would "look like Khan Younis" — a reference to the southern Gaza city devastated during Israel's campaign against Hamas.

On the ground in Beirut, the humanitarian situation is deteriorating rapidly. Nearly 96,000 people have officially registered at displacement centers across Lebanon, but volunteers report shelters are already at capacity and food supplies are dwindling. At the National Theatre in Beirut, mattresses line the balcony above the stage. Mohamed Baydoun, a 73-year-old who fled the southern city of Tyre, told reporters he had survived several wars but that this one felt "different" — as though "only one side will be left standing." A mother who escaped with her two-month-old baby said she had managed to grab diapers but forgotten milk in the rush to evacuate.

Lebanese Prime Minister Nawaf Salam warned foreign ambassadors on Friday that "a humanitarian disaster is looming," calling the political and humanitarian consequences potentially "unprecedented." French President Emmanuel Macron echoed the alarm, describing the situation as a moment of "great danger" and urging Hezbollah to cease fire toward Israel while calling on Israel to refrain from any ground intervention or large-scale military operation on Lebanese soil. Reports from the border region indicated Israeli troops and tanks were massing, fueling speculation about a possible full-scale ground incursion.

For civilians caught in the crossfire, the calculus is simpler and more desperate. Fourteen-year-old Dunia Hassaballaa, who fled Dahieh with her parents amid gridlocked traffic on Thursday, spent the night unable to sleep, watching the news to check whether her home had been hit. "It's way too early for Lebanon to have another war," she said. At a Beirut bookshop café that had provided free meals during the previous conflict, operations manager Khodor Al-Akhdar was once again preparing food for displaced families breaking their Ramadan fast — but with little optimism. "We don't have the energy," he said. "Mentally we're not good, physically we're not good from the last war."

articles.headline: Middle East Conflict Triggers Scramble for Alternative Energy Export Routes
articles.subheadline: Widening Iran war forces Gulf exporters to seek new pathways as refineries cut output amid supply disruption fears
articles.body:
The escalating military conflict involving Iran is sending shockwaves through global energy markets, as major Gulf exporters race to secure alternative routes for oil and gas shipments while refineries across the region scale back production amid mounting security concerns.

The widening hostilities have raised the specter of a significant global energy crunch, with the Persian Gulf — the chokepoint through which roughly one-fifth of the world's petroleum supply flows daily — now at the center of an active conflict zone. Energy producers that have long relied on established maritime corridors through the Strait of Hormuz and surrounding waterways are being forced to evaluate overland pipelines, alternative port facilities, and longer shipping routes to maintain deliveries to global customers.

The scramble for new export pathways underscores the fragility of the world's energy supply architecture, which remains heavily dependent on Middle Eastern production and Gulf transit routes. A number of refineries in the region have already begun reducing their output, adding a further layer of supply pressure at a time when global inventories offer limited cushion. The production cuts, driven by both physical security risks and logistical bottlenecks, threaten to tighten fuel markets in Asia and Europe, where Gulf crude and refined products are critical inputs.

For energy-importing nations, the disruption revives long-standing debates about supply diversification and strategic reserve adequacy. Countries across Asia — particularly China, India, Japan, and South Korea — face the most acute exposure given their outsized dependence on Gulf crude. European buyers, still recalibrating supply chains in the wake of earlier Russian energy disruptions, confront the prospect of yet another major source of uncertainty.

The conflict's impact on energy infrastructure and trade flows will likely accelerate investment in bypass pipelines and alternative export terminals that have been discussed for years but never built with full urgency. In the near term, however, markets face the reality that no quick substitutes exist for the volume of hydrocarbons that transit the Gulf, leaving prices vulnerable to further spikes if the military situation continues to deteriorate.

articles.headline: Tehran Residents Report Heaviest Night of Strikes as Iran War Intensifies
articles.subheadline: Civilians describe shaking buildings, sleepless nights, and a capital transformed into a 'ghost town' amid US-Israeli bombardment
articles.body:
Residents of Tehran have described Thursday night as the most devastating round of airstrikes since the United States and Israel launched their military campaign against Iran six days ago, with continuous explosions shaking buildings across the capital and surrounding areas for hours.

Accounts gathered by BBC Persian paint a picture of a city under siege. One man in his 30s reported that his house shook for five minutes without pause, calling it "the worst night" since strikes began. A woman in her 40s said explosions jolted her awake at 5:00 a.m. and made further sleep impossible. In Damavand, a city near Tehran where some families have fled for safety, a young man described seeing an explosion for the first time — a light shooting skyward as every window in his home rattled. His family packed two cars to evacuate. The US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency (HRANA) has reported 1,168 civilian deaths since the conflict began, including 194 children, though the BBC has been unable to independently verify casualty figures from inside Iran.

Perhaps most striking is the divided mood among ordinary Iranians. Some residents told the BBC they sense a current of support for the strikes among citizens exhausted by decades of rule under the Islamic Republic. One young woman in Tehran said people climb onto rooftops to watch the bombardment, waving at passing fighter jets and whistling. "The eighth and ninth of January were much scarier for them than this past week," she said, referencing dates when Iranian security forces violently suppressed anti-government protests. "Back then, we ourselves were the targets of the bullets." She added that some residents openly say they want the strikes to continue "until it's finished by Nowruz" — the Persian New Year on March 20.

Not all share that sentiment. Others expressed deep anxiety about what comes after the bombing stops. "We mustn't end up like Iraq or Syria — honestly, we don't deserve that," one young man said, invoking the devastating civil wars that consumed both countries. He described Tehran as a ghost town reeking of gunpowder, with checkpoints at every few intersections and pro-government loyalists blasting slogans from cars with loudspeakers. Darkness and shuttered storefronts define the streetscape. "I hope things change — people live on hope," he said. "But if it doesn't happen this time, then it probably never will."

The intensifying strikes come as the broader conflict sends shockwaves through global markets and transportation networks. Jet fuel prices have surged more than 80 percent due to disruption of Gulf supplies, Qatar has warned that all Gulf oil production could halt, and airlines are scrambling to reroute flights as Iran-related airspace closures expand. International reporting from inside Iran remains severely constrained, with authorities routinely denying visas to foreign journalists and imposing internet blackouts that limit the flow of information from the country.

articles.headline: Qatar Puts 10 LNG Tankers Up for Lease Amid Prolonged Export Shutdown
articles.subheadline: The world's top LNG exporter seeks to redeploy idle fleet as Middle East conflict keeps key facility offline
articles.body:
Qatar is offering 10 liquefied natural gas tankers under its control for lease to outside parties, a striking signal that the Gulf state sees no near-term resolution to the conflict that has shuttered its primary export facility in the Persian Gulf.

The move, reported by Bloomberg on Thursday, underscores the cascading economic consequences of the ongoing Middle East war on global energy infrastructure. Qatar, one of the world's largest LNG exporters, has been unable to operate its massive export terminal due to the hostilities, leaving a fleet of purpose-built carriers without cargo to haul. Rather than absorb the mounting costs of idle vessels, Doha is now seeking to lease them on the open market — a pragmatic but symbolically significant acknowledgment that its export operations face an extended interruption.

The availability of 10 additional tankers could provide a modest supply-side relief to the global LNG shipping market, where charter rates have fluctuated amid shifting trade flows caused by the war. However, the broader implication is less reassuring for energy markets: Qatar's decision to redeploy rather than hold its fleet in reserve suggests that planners in Doha do not expect a rapid restart of exports. Qatar's LNG output is a critical source of supply for buyers across Asia and Europe, and its prolonged absence from the market has forced importers to scramble for alternative cargoes, often at premium prices.

The conflict's disruption of Qatari exports has added a volatile dimension to an already tight global gas market. European nations, which accelerated their pivot to LNG following the severing of Russian pipeline supplies, have been particularly exposed. Asian buyers, many of whom hold long-term contracts with Qatar, face uncertainty over delivery timelines and potential force majeure claims.

For Qatar, the tanker leasing strategy represents a calculated effort to limit financial losses while preserving optionality. The vessels — highly specialized and expensive to maintain — generate no revenue sitting idle. By placing them into the charter market, Qatar can offset carrying costs while retaining the ability to recall ships when exports eventually resume. The question now facing global energy markets is how long that wait will be.

articles.headline: Mandelson's Bail Conditions Dropped but Investigation Continues
articles.subheadline: Former UK ambassador to the US had his passport returned after arrest on suspicion of misconduct in public office tied to Epstein emails
articles.body:
Lord Peter Mandelson, the former British ambassador to the United States, has had all bail conditions lifted and his passport returned by the Metropolitan Police, though he remains under active investigation for suspected misconduct in public office. The development, confirmed Friday by both the Met and Mandelson's legal team at Mishcon de Reya, comes roughly one week after his arrest on February 23.

The 72-year-old Labour grandee was detained over allegations that he passed market-sensitive government information to the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein while serving as a UK minister. The arrest followed a tip-off from House of Commons Speaker Sir Lindsay Hoyle, who reportedly alerted police to concerns that Mandelson might be planning to travel to the British Virgin Islands. The Met subsequently apologized to Hoyle for inadvertently revealing him as the source of the information. Mandelson's lawyers have dismissed the suggestion he intended to flee the country as "baseless," and said Friday that their client "did not and does not pose a flight risk."

The allegations trace back to a tranche of documents released last month by the US Department of Justice, which included email exchanges between Mandelson and Epstein. The revelations triggered a political crisis that has rippled through Downing Street. Mandelson was appointed ambassador to Washington in February 2025 but was dismissed in September after the government said newly surfaced details about the depth of his relationship with Epstein could no longer be overlooked. Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer has faced sustained questioning about what he knew of the Epstein connection when he made the appointment, and his chief of staff, Morgan McSweeney, resigned over the affair.

Mandelson's position, according to sources familiar with his thinking, is that he committed no criminal act and was not motivated by financial gain. His lawyers said he would continue to cooperate with the police investigation but would not comment further. The Met confirmed only that he had been "released under investigation" and that inquiries were ongoing.

The case has added to a broader reckoning over Epstein's network of relationships with powerful figures on both sides of the Atlantic. In Washington, the US Congress recently voted to summon Attorney General Pam Bondi in connection with the Epstein case, while in Britain, Prince Andrew has separately given up the lease on a Crown Estate property amid continued scrutiny of his own ties to the disgraced financier.

articles.headline: Trump Demands Iran's 'Unconditional Surrender' as Airstrikes Continue
articles.subheadline: President rules out negotiated resolution, escalating pressure on Tehran amid ongoing US and Israeli military operations
articles.body:
President Donald Trump has declared he will not pursue a negotiated settlement with Iran, instead demanding the country's unconditional surrender as joint US and Israeli airstrikes continue to pound targets across the nation.

The president made the pronouncement via social media, marking a dramatic hardening of Washington's position and effectively closing the door on diplomatic channels that various international actors had been working to establish. The demand echoes the most uncompromising wartime rhetoric in modern American presidential history, language historically reserved for conflicts approaching their terminal phase.

The insistence on capitulation rather than negotiation represents a significant escalation in both tone and strategy. Unconditional surrender demands leave no room for the face-saving compromises that have historically enabled adversaries to come to the table, raising questions about how and when the conflict might reach a conclusion. Military analysts have long warned that such maximalist positions can extend hostilities by eliminating incentives for an opponent to stop fighting.

The ongoing US and Israeli air campaign has been sustained over a period that has tested both military resources and international patience. While the Trump administration has framed the operations as necessary for regional security and the neutralization of Iran's nuclear and ballistic missile capabilities, the conflict has drawn sharp criticism from humanitarian organizations and a number of US allies who have called for restraint and a return to diplomacy.

Markets have been closely tracking the trajectory of the conflict, with energy prices remaining elevated amid persistent concerns about supply disruptions in the Persian Gulf region. Defense sector equities have broadly outperformed since hostilities began, while broader indices have reflected investor unease over the geopolitical uncertainty. Trump's latest comments are likely to further dampen hopes for a near-term resolution, potentially extending the risk premium that has been priced into global oil markets and weighing on sentiment across asset classes that are sensitive to prolonged instability in the Middle East.

articles.headline: Brent Crude Surges to $90 as Hormuz Strait Shipping Grinds to Halt
articles.subheadline: Middle East conflict chokes the world's most critical oil chokepoint, sending energy markets into turmoil
articles.body:
Brent crude futures surged past $90 per barrel on Thursday for the first time in nearly two years, as escalating military conflict in the Middle East brought shipping through the Strait of Hormuz to a near-complete standstill and sent shockwaves through global energy markets.

The strait, a narrow waterway between Iran and Oman through which roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil consumption flows, has long been considered the single most strategically sensitive chokepoint in global energy infrastructure. Its effective closure represents the kind of supply disruption scenario that energy analysts have warned about for decades but that markets have rarely been forced to price in at this magnitude. Approximately 20 million barrels of oil per day typically transit the passage, serving as the primary export route for crude from Saudi Arabia, Iraq, the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, and Qatar.

The price spike marks a dramatic reversal for oil markets, which had spent much of the past two years trading in a relatively subdued range amid concerns about sluggish global demand and ample supply from non-OPEC producers. Brent had last traded at $90 in mid-2024, and the benchmark's rapid ascent underscores how quickly geopolitical risk can overwhelm fundamental supply-and-demand dynamics. West Texas Intermediate crude also climbed sharply in sympathy with the global benchmark.

The disruption poses immediate economic consequences well beyond the energy sector. Higher crude prices feed directly into gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel costs, threatening to reignite inflationary pressures that central banks in the United States and Europe have spent years trying to tame. For energy-importing nations across Asia — particularly China, Japan, South Korea, and India, which depend heavily on Gulf crude — the supply interruption could force a scramble for alternative sourcing and draw down strategic petroleum reserves.

Traders and analysts are now watching closely for any diplomatic or military developments that could either restore transit through Hormuz or further entrench the blockage. Several major shipping companies have already rerouted tankers, and insurance premiums for vessels operating in the Persian Gulf have skyrocketed. Should the disruption persist, market participants warn that Brent could test significantly higher levels, with some forecasters pointing to $100 or beyond if the conflict shows no signs of resolution in the coming weeks.

articles.headline: Physicists Map Global Settlement Patterns Using Percolation Theory
articles.subheadline: New dataset identifies the critical distances at which isolated human settlements merge into vast interconnected clusters
articles.body:
An international team of researchers has applied percolation theory — a concept rooted in statistical physics — to map the spatial configuration of human settlements across the entire globe, producing what they call the Global Settlement Percolation (GSP) dataset.

The study, led by Martin Schorcht and Diego Rybski along with seven collaborators, introduces a method for characterizing how settlements are arranged across landscapes by identifying the "critical distance" at which isolated towns, villages, and cities effectively merge into a single giant cluster. Percolation theory, long used in physics to describe how connectivity emerges in random systems — from fluid flowing through porous rock to the spread of forest fires — here finds a striking application in quantifying the structure of human land use. The researchers computed these critical distances at multiple spatial scales, from national and subnational administrative units to non-overlapping grid tiles and moving windows, covering every inhabited region on Earth.

The critical distance serves as an independent metric of settlement connectivity, distinct from conventional measures like population density or urban footprint area. A region with a short critical distance has settlements packed closely enough that they readily form contiguous urban corridors, while a longer critical distance suggests more dispersed, isolated communities. This single parameter encodes rich information about how human habitation is distributed — information that the authors argue carries significant implications for urban planning, ecological conservation, and land-use policy.

The work sits at the intersection of physics and social science, classified under both "Physics and Society" and "Data Analysis, Statistics and Probability" on the arXiv preprint server where it was posted. The researchers note that a "substantial share of the Earth's land surface is managed by humans, with cities representing the most extreme form of anthropogenic land use," and that the virtually infinite ways settlements can be arranged have profound consequences for both urban systems and the natural environment.

The GSP dataset is designed to serve a broad range of research communities, including those focused on urban morphology, landscape ecology, and the social and economic consequences of settlement structure. By reducing the complex geometry of global human habitation to a physically meaningful threshold, the work offers a powerful new lens for understanding how humanity organizes itself across the planet — and what that organization means for the ecosystems it displaces.

articles.headline: TriZetto Data Breach Exposes 3.4 Million Patients' Health Records
articles.subheadline: The Cognizant-owned health tech firm took nearly a year to detect hackers who infiltrated its servers in late 2024
articles.body:
Health technology company TriZetto has disclosed that a cyberattack compromised the personal and medical data of more than 3.4 million individuals, in a breach that went undetected for roughly eleven months. The company, a subsidiary of multinational IT services firm Cognizant, filed notice with Maine's attorney general on Friday detailing the scope of the intrusion.

TriZetto said it discovered the breach on October 2, 2025, but determined that hackers had maintained access to its systems since November 2024. The stolen data includes patients' names, dates of birth, home addresses, Social Security numbers, healthcare provider names, demographic information, and insurance details — all extracted from insurance eligibility transaction reports stored on the company's servers. The company has not publicly explained why the intrusion evaded detection for so long.

The breach carries outsized significance given TriZetto's central role in U.S. healthcare infrastructure. According to its website, the platform serves approximately 200 million people across 875,000 healthcare providers nationwide, functioning as a critical intermediary that helps doctors' offices and medical facilities verify patients' insurance coverage. Among the organizations confirming their patients were affected is OCHIN, a nonprofit that provides health technology services to roughly 300 rural and community care providers. Several California-based healthcare providers have also acknowledged exposure, though TriZetto noted that not all of its customers were impacted.

Cognizant did not immediately respond to requests for comment. The company has not disclosed whether the attack involved ransomware or identified the threat actors responsible.

The incident adds to a troubling pattern of large-scale breaches targeting health technology firms. In 2024, a ransomware attack on Change Healthcare — which processes approximately 15 billion healthcare transactions annually — resulted in the theft of more than 192 million patient records and triggered widespread outages that left patients across the country temporarily unable to access treatments and medications. The TriZetto breach, while smaller in scale, underscores persistent vulnerabilities in the digital infrastructure underpinning American healthcare, where centralized platforms handling vast troves of sensitive data remain high-value targets for cybercriminals.

articles.headline: FBI Probes Breach of Its Own Wiretap and Surveillance Systems
articles.subheadline: The hack reportedly compromised networks used to manage wiretaps and foreign intelligence warrants, adding to a growing list of U.S. government cyber intrusions.
articles.body:
The Federal Bureau of Investigation is actively investigating a breach of its own internal networks, specifically systems used to manage court-authorized wiretaps and foreign intelligence surveillance warrants, according to a report first disclosed by CNN on Thursday.

An FBI spokesperson confirmed to reporters that the agency "identified and addressed suspicious activities on FBI networks" and has "leveraged all technical capabilities to respond," but declined to elaborate on the scope of the intrusion or identify the suspected perpetrators. The compromised systems are among the most sensitive in U.S. law enforcement, handling the technical infrastructure that enables real-time monitoring of communications targets under judicial authorization.

The breach lands amid what has become a relentless drumbeat of cyberattacks against American government institutions and critical infrastructure. Over the past year alone, Chinese state-linked hackers penetrated the U.S. Treasury Department and the National Nuclear Security Administration, the agency responsible for the security of the nation's nuclear arsenal. Russian-affiliated threat actors separately stole sealed records from the U.S. Courts' electronic filing system. Perhaps most consequentially, the Chinese government hacking group known as Salt Typhoon compromised at least 200 American companies, according to the FBI's own accounting — including major telecommunications carriers AT&T, Verizon, Lumen, Charter Communications, and Windstream.

The FBI wiretap system breach raises particularly acute concerns because it could potentially reveal the identities of surveillance targets, the scope of active investigations, and the methods used by federal agents to monitor suspected criminals and foreign intelligence operatives. If a nation-state actor is behind the intrusion, the intelligence value of such access would be extraordinary — offering a foreign adversary a window into precisely which of its operatives or assets the United States is actively tracking.

The incident also arrives at a moment of broader tension over the security of government data. Critics have raised alarms about Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency gaining access to sensitive federal databases in ways that allegedly violated longstanding security protocols, with at least one U.S. senator warning that the arrangement posed national security risks. Taken together, the cascade of breaches paints a picture of a federal cybersecurity posture under severe and sustained stress, with the nation's most sensitive systems proving vulnerable to increasingly sophisticated adversaries.

articles.headline: Israel Claims Destruction of Most Iranian Missile Launchers
articles.subheadline: IDF says strikes have significantly reduced volume of ballistic missiles targeting Israel and regional allies
articles.body:
The Israel Defense Forces announced on Thursday that it has destroyed the majority of Iran's ballistic-missile launch infrastructure, marking what it described as a significant degradation of Tehran's capacity to strike Israel and neighboring countries.

According to the IDF, the sustained campaign against Iranian missile assets has produced measurable results over the course of the week, with the number of missiles being fired toward Israel and other regional targets declining steadily. The military did not specify exact figures for the launchers destroyed or the percentage reduction in incoming fire, but characterized the damage to Iran's launch capability as substantial.

The claim, if verified, would represent a major shift in the military balance between the two longtime adversaries. Iran has spent decades building one of the largest ballistic-missile arsenals in the Middle East, with systems capable of reaching targets across the region. The destruction of a majority of its launcher infrastructure would significantly limit Tehran's ability to sustain prolonged missile barrages and could reshape the strategic calculus for both sides.

The announcement comes amid an intensifying conflict that has drawn in multiple actors across the Middle East. Energy markets and global defense stocks have been closely tracking developments, with investors weighing the potential for further escalation against the possibility that degraded Iranian capabilities could accelerate a path toward de-escalation. Oil prices have remained volatile as traders assess the risk to regional energy infrastructure and shipping lanes.

Independent confirmation of the IDF's claims has not yet been available, and Iran has not immediately responded to the assertions. Military analysts have cautioned that mobile launcher platforms can be difficult to locate and destroy comprehensively, and that Iran may retain hidden or dispersed assets. Nonetheless, a sustained drop in missile launches would lend credibility to Israel's account and could influence diplomatic efforts to contain the conflict.

articles.headline: Satellite Images Reveal Multiple Strikes Hit Iran School and Military Base
articles.subheadline: BBC Verify analysis shows at least five impact sites around Minab primary school where Iran says 168 people died
articles.body:
Satellite imagery captured days after a devastating attack in southern Iran reveals that a primary school and an adjacent Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps compound were struck by multiple munitions in what appears to have been a coordinated assault, according to analysis by BBC Verify and independent weapons experts.

The attack on the Shajareh Tayebeh primary school in Minab on Saturday killed 168 people, according to Iranian authorities, who say the majority of victims were children. The school had 264 enrolled pupils, Iran's Education Ministry reported. Satellite images taken on March 4 by Planet Labs show at least five buildings with visible craters and scorch marks, with one structure inside the IRGC base completely flattened and the school building partially collapsed. Munitions expert N.R. Jenzen Jones of Armament Research Services said the pattern of damage suggests "multiple simultaneous or near-simultaneous strikes," while a McKenzie Intelligence Services analyst noted that a crater on the school's ground floor indicates a specialized penetrating munition may have been used.

Iranian officials have blamed the United States and Israel for the attack, but neither has claimed responsibility. Israel stated it was "not aware" of any operations in the area and is looking into the incident. U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told the BBC on Wednesday that Washington is still investigating, insisting the U.S. would "never target civilian targets." However, during the same news conference, an illustrative map of the "First 100 Hours" of the U.S.-Israeli military campaign against Iran showed strike locations along the southern coast, which includes the Minab area. Without recovered munition fragments, experts say definitive attribution remains impossible.

Verified footage from the scene shows harrowing aftermath — rescue workers pulling through rubble as families wail, children's bloodied backpacks and schoolbooks scattered among debris, and in one widely shared clip, a child's severed arm recovered beneath collapsed concrete. A handwritten list published by Iranian media names 56 of the dead, 48 of whom were between six and eleven years old. Aerial footage taken three days later showed at least 100 freshly dug graves arranged in rows. The U.S.-based Human Rights News Agency has documented 1,114 Iranian civilian deaths since the war began, including 183 children.

The school's proximity to the IRGC compound has fueled speculation about the intended target. Satellite imagery from 2013 shows the school building was originally part of the same compound before a separating wall appeared by 2016. Oregon State University satellite analyst Jamon Van den Hoek told the BBC the clustering of impact sites "suggests there were one or more targets in close proximity," adding that the strikes appeared intentional but that the specific intended target remains unclear. An ongoing internet blackout across Iran has severely limited the flow of ground-level evidence, making satellite analysis and previously verified social media footage the primary means of understanding the scale of the destruction.

articles.headline: Fed's Waller Flags War With Iran as Inflation Risk Amid Tariff Uncertainty
articles.subheadline: Federal Reserve governor weighs in on geopolitical threats to price stability, labor market outlook, and private credit
articles.body:
Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller warned that a potential military conflict with Iran could reignite inflationary pressures in the U.S. economy, adding another layer of uncertainty to an already complex monetary policy landscape shaped by trade tensions and a cooling labor market.

Speaking in remarks covered by Bloomberg on March 6, Waller outlined how a war involving Iran — a major oil-producing nation — could disrupt global energy markets and send commodity prices surging, complicating the Fed's efforts to bring inflation sustainably back to its 2% target. Oil supply disruptions in the Persian Gulf region have historically triggered sharp price spikes that ripple through transportation, manufacturing, and consumer goods sectors. With the Fed already navigating the inflationary fallout from ongoing tariff policies, a geopolitical shock of that magnitude would significantly narrow the central bank's room to maneuver on interest rates.

Waller also addressed the state of the U.S. labor market, which has shown signs of gradual softening in recent months. While payrolls data has remained broadly resilient, the pace of hiring has decelerated, and policymakers are watching closely for signs that the jobs market could weaken more sharply. The interplay between a still-tight labor market and persistent price pressures has kept the Fed cautious about the timing and pace of any future rate adjustments.

On tariffs, Waller acknowledged the ongoing risks that trade policy poses to both inflation and economic growth. The uncertainty surrounding the scope and duration of tariffs has made it difficult for businesses to plan and invest, while higher import costs continue to feed through to consumer prices. The Fed has repeatedly signaled that trade-related inflation is among the hardest variables to model, given its dependence on political decisions rather than economic fundamentals.

Waller also weighed in on the rapid expansion of private credit markets, which have grown substantially as traditional banks have pulled back from certain lending activities. While private credit has filled an important gap in corporate financing, regulators have flagged concerns about transparency, valuation practices, and the potential for systemic risk as the sector scales. Waller's comments suggest the Fed is monitoring the space closely, even as it remains outside the direct purview of traditional bank supervision. His wide-ranging remarks underscore the unusually crowded set of risks facing U.S. monetary policymakers as they chart a course through 2026.

articles.headline: Kazakhstan Central Bank to Channel $350M in Reserves Into Digital Assets
articles.subheadline: The allocation targets crypto infrastructure firms and index funds, representing a small fraction of the nation's $69.4 billion reserves
articles.body:
Kazakhstan's central bank announced plans to invest up to $350 million from its gold and foreign exchange reserves into assets tied to cryptocurrencies and digital infrastructure — a notable step for a sovereign monetary authority, even as officials emphasized the move's modest scale.

Governor Timur Suleimanov said the National Bank of Kazakhstan is assembling a list of eligible investments that will go beyond direct cryptocurrency holdings. Instead, the strategy will target shares of high-tech companies connected to the digital asset ecosystem, cryptocurrency infrastructure firms, and index funds that track crypto market performance. Deputy chair Aliya Moldabekova told Reuters the investments are expected to be deployed in April and May 2026. "We are not talking about any large investment in cryptocurrencies," Moldabekova said. "We are currently selecting companies that deal with digital assets, for example those involved in cryptocurrency infrastructure."

The $350 million allocation amounts to roughly 0.5% of the central bank's $69.4 billion in gold and foreign exchange reserves as of February 1. Kazakhstan also maintains a separate national fund, built from oil revenues, with $65.23 billion in assets — placing the proposed digital asset bet in perspective as a cautious, exploratory move rather than a dramatic pivot.

The announcement reflects Kazakhstan's deepening ties to the crypto sector. The Central Asian nation emerged as one of the world's leading bitcoin mining hubs after China banned the practice in 2021, sending operators scrambling for new jurisdictions with cheap electricity. In 2025, Astana-based Fonte Capital launched Central Asia's first physically backed spot bitcoin exchange-traded fund, further cementing the country's position as a regional digital asset center.

While a handful of sovereign wealth funds and state-linked entities have gained indirect exposure to cryptocurrencies through equity holdings — most notably through shares of firms like MicroStrategy or Coinbase — explicit central bank allocation to crypto-linked assets remains rare globally. Kazakhstan's approach, focused on infrastructure companies and index products rather than direct token purchases, appears designed to capture upside from the sector's growth while managing volatility risk. The move will be closely watched by other emerging-market central banks weighing their own exposure to digital assets as the sector continues to mature and attract institutional capital.

articles.headline: Analyst: IRGC Power Structure Must Be Dismantled for Iranian Self-Rule
articles.subheadline: Following Khamenei's death in US-Israeli conflict, shadow state apparatus works to prevent power vacuum, researcher warns
articles.body:
The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps and the office of Iran's Supreme Leader are maneuvering behind the scenes to maintain control of the country following the death of Ali Khamenei in the US-Israeli war, according to a leading researcher on Iranian power structures.

Kasra Aarabi, Director of IRGC Research at United Against Nuclear Iran (UANI), told Bloomberg Television on March 6 that Iran's "invisible" state — the intertwined apparatus of the Supreme Leader's office and the IRGC — is actively working to ensure continuity of the existing power structure and prevent any vacuum that could open space for popular governance. Aarabi argued that ordinary Iranians will be unable to meaningfully determine the direction of their country unless this deeply entrenched institutional framework is fully dismantled.

The succession question in Tehran has become one of the most consequential geopolitical uncertainties following Khamenei's death. The Supreme Leader sat atop a complex web of military, economic, and religious authority that extended far beyond formal government institutions. The IRGC, in particular, controls vast commercial enterprises, commands paramilitary and intelligence operations, and wields influence across the Middle East through proxy forces in Lebanon, Iraq, Syria, and Yemen. Analysts have long noted that Iran effectively operates as a dual state, with elected civilian officials holding limited power relative to the unelected bodies loyal to the Supreme Leader.

Aarabi's assessment underscores concerns among Western policymakers and Iranian diaspora communities that the post-Khamenei transition could simply reproduce authoritarian control under new leadership rather than creating an opening for democratic reform. The IRGC's deep integration into Iran's economy — with interests spanning construction, telecommunications, and energy — gives it powerful institutional incentives to resist any fundamental restructuring of the political order.

The remarks come as governments in Washington, London, and across the European Union closely monitor Tehran for signals about who will consolidate power and what posture the next leadership configuration will adopt on nuclear negotiations, regional policy, and domestic governance. For now, the apparatus Khamenei built over more than three decades appears intent on surviving its founder.

articles.headline: Bitcoin Slides Toward $70,000 as Iran War and Jobs Data Spook Markets
articles.subheadline: Surging oil prices, a strengthening dollar, and looming U.S. payroll figures drive broad risk-off sentiment across crypto and equities
articles.body:
Bitcoin fell sharply toward $70,000 on Friday, retreating from a weekly high of $74,000 as the convergence of escalating Middle East conflict and a pivotal U.S. employment report sent investors scrambling for safer ground.

The world's largest cryptocurrency was trading at roughly $69,921 as of mid-morning Friday, down more than 3.5% over the prior 24 hours. The pullback came as the war with Iran entered the closing days of its first week, sending West Texas Intermediate crude surging above $83 per barrel — a gain of more than 5% in a single day. Rising energy costs have reignited inflation fears, complicating the outlook for Federal Reserve monetary policy and dampening appetite for speculative assets.

Markets were also bracing for the Bureau of Labor Statistics' March nonfarm payrolls report, due at 13:30 UTC. Economists expected payroll growth to slow to just 59,000 jobs, with the unemployment rate holding steady at 4.3%. A weak print could bolster the case for rate cuts, while a surprise to the upside might reinforce the Fed's cautious stance. The uncertainty alone was enough to trigger defensive positioning: the U.S. Dollar Index climbed above 99, the 10-year Treasury yield rose to approximately 4.16%, and the Nasdaq-100-tracking Invesco QQQ ETF slipped about 0.5% in pre-market trading.

Crypto-linked equities mirrored bitcoin's weakness. Strategy (MSTR), Coinbase (COIN), and MARA Holdings (MARA) all traded lower ahead of the opening bell. Derivatives data pointed to cautious institutional positioning, with rising open interest accompanied by increased short hedging and options markets pricing in a near-term volatility event. Separately, oil's climb to as high as $85 per barrel prompted traders to begin pricing the possibility of a European Central Bank rate increase — a scenario that would further tighten global liquidity conditions unfavorable to risk assets.

The selloff marks a notable reversal from earlier in the week, when bitcoin briefly touched $74,000 on optimism around regulatory developments, including a settlement between the SEC and Tron founder Justin Sun and Ripple's expansion of its clearing platform. Those tailwinds, however, have been overwhelmed by the macroeconomic and geopolitical headwinds now dominating trader sentiment. If bitcoin breaks decisively below $70,000, it would mark the first sustained trading beneath that level since Wednesday and could open the door to further downside pressure in an already jittery market.

articles.headline: Cluely CEO Roy Lee Admits He Fabricated Revenue Figures Shared With Press
articles.subheadline: The startup founder claims the $7M ARR figure was offhand, but emails show the interview was arranged by his own PR team
articles.body:
Roy Lee, the co-founder and CEO of AI startup Cluely, publicly acknowledged on Thursday that revenue numbers he provided to TechCrunch last summer were fabricated — but his account of how the figures came to be published appears to contradict the documented record.

In a post on X, Lee admitted that the $7 million in annual recurring revenue he cited during a June 2025 interview was false, calling it "the only blatantly dishonest thing I've said publicly online." He shared Stripe account screenshots suggesting the company's actual numbers at the time were roughly $5.2 million in combined consumer and enterprise ARR, with a run rate of approximately $6.3 million — meaningful figures, but well short of the $7 million he originally claimed. Lee characterized the interview as a "random cold call from some woman" about numbers, saying he offered fabricated figures without expecting them to be published.

That characterization does not match the paper trail. According to TechCrunch, a public relations representative working for Cluely proactively pitched the interview via email on June 27, 2025, offering to make Lee available for a story. The reporter agreed, the PR representative provided Lee's phone number, confirmed he was expecting the call, and the interview proceeded as arranged. The discrepancy between Lee's version of events and the documented exchange raises questions about the founder's credibility beyond the revenue fabrication itself.

The admission is particularly notable given Cluely's trajectory and investor profile. The company, which originally gained notoriety as a tool that let users secretly look up answers during video calls, raised a $5.3 million seed round from Abstract Ventures and Susa Ventures, followed by a $15 million Series A led by Andreessen Horowitz in June 2025. Whether Cluely's investors were aware of the inflated figures — or whether the public claims influenced subsequent fundraising — remains unclear. Fabricating revenue figures shared with the press, while not equivalent to securities fraud, can carry serious reputational and legal implications, particularly if investors relied on public reporting when making funding decisions.

Ironically, Lee appeared to foreshadow the controversy during a panel at TechCrunch Disrupt in October 2025, when he told the audience, "What I've learned is you should never share revenue numbers." Cluely has since pivoted from its controversial cheating-tool origins to position itself as an AI-powered meeting note-taker. Whether the company can similarly pivot past its founder's credibility crisis is an open question. Neither Lee nor Cluely's investors have publicly commented on whether internal disclosures to backers were also affected by the misrepresentation.
